mnemonics.title: PCA: Principal Components Align
mnemonics.content: In Principal Component Analysis (PCA), the axes (Principal Components) are aligned in the direction of maximum variance. Mnemonic: 'Principal Components Align with the longest axis of the data cloud.'

mnemonics.title: t-SNE: Tiny Stars Near Each Other
mnemonics.content: t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding (t-SNE) aims to keep similar points clustered together in the low-dimensional map. Mnemonic: 'Tiny Stars Near Each Other' (representing the clusters).

mnemonics.title: The Swiss Roll Unrolling
mnemonics.content: The classic 'Swiss Roll' dataset is a 2D plane rolled up in 3D space. Manifold learning algorithms like Isomap try to 'unroll' this to find the true 2D structure without cutting the paper.

mnemonics.title: UMAP: You Map A Patch
mnemonics.content: Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection (UMAP) uses fuzzy topology. Mnemonic: 'You Map A Patch'—it looks at local patches (neighbors) to construct a topological structure and then projects it to lower dimensions.

acronyms.usage: Linear dimensionality reduction. Find the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix to project data onto orthogonal axes of maximum variance.
Non-linear technique for visualization (2D/3D). Uses a Student t-distribution to compute probabilities of similarity to keep local structure and avoid crowding.

Non-linear dimensionality reduction. Uses algebraic topology to build a fuzzy topological representation and optimize the low-dimensional embedding faster than t-SNE while preserving global structure.
Assumes data lies on a manifold. It reconstructs each point from a linear combination of its nearest neighbors to preserve local geometry in the lower dimension.
Estimates the geodesic (curved surface) distance between points on a manifold (e.g., along the curve of a Swiss roll) and applies MDS using these distances.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a garden hose. To an ant walking on it, the hose is effectively a 1D line (intrinsic dimension), even though it exists in 3D space. This is the manifold hypothesis—data has a low 'intrinsic' dimension hidden in high-dimensional space.

Visualize a stretched oval (ellipsoid) of data points. The first Principal Component is the longest axis of the oval. The second is the longest axis perpendicular to the first. They are always orthogonal (90 degrees).

Think of a map of the world (Mercator projection). The globe is 3D, the map is 2D. Distortions occur. Manifold learning tries to find the 'best' flat map that preserves the relationships of the curved surface.

PCA is like shining a flashlight (projection) to cast a shadow on a wall—straight lines remain straight. Manifold learning is like bending a piece of wire into a loop; the algorithm tries to unbend it back to a straight line.

analogies.analogy: A movie trailer. A 2-hour movie is high-dimensional data. The trailer reduces it to 2 minutes (low dimension) but tries to keep the essential plot (structure) and key characters (clusters) intact.

A crumpled ball of paper. The ball looks complex and high-dimensional (every crease), but it is fundamentally just a 2D sheet of paper. Machine learning tries to 'smooth out' the crumples to find the simple sheet.

Finding a specific grain of rice in a giant spherical storage silo. As the silo gets bigger (dimensions increase), the volume is so vast that the rice is effectively lost, and distance measurements between grains become meaningless.

Think of perplexity as the 'window size' of context you are looking at. Low perplexity means looking at just your immediate neighbors (focus on local details). High perplexity means looking at a larger group (focus on broader trends).
